lecture.New_Day4Part1audio01.mp3: SQL supports standard arithmetic operators that you can use in SELECT expressions, WHERE conditions, and ORDER BY clauses. They operate on numeric data types.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio02.mp3: Now, let's look at the five core arithmetic operators in action. First, the plus sign performs addition — like adding a ten-thousand bonus to a base salary. Next, the minus sign handles subtraction — deducting five thousand for expenses. The asterisk is used for multiplication — like applying a ten percent salary raise with one-point-one. The forward slash performs division — dividing annual salary by twelve to find monthly pay. And finally, the percent sign is the modulo operator — which gives you the remainder left over after division.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio03.mp3: Here are arithmetic operators in action. In the first query, we divide salary by twelve point zero for monthly pay, and multiply by zero point ten for a ten percent bonus. In the second, we subtract cost price from unit price to calculate gross profit on the fly.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio04.mp3: Watch out for integer division! In SQLite, dividing two whole numbers truncates the decimal — so seven divided by two equals three. To get exact decimals, always include a decimal point like twelve point zero.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio05.mp3: When combining multiple operators in a query, SQL follows strict precedence rules. Higher precedence operators are evaluated first, determining how expressions are parsed.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio06.mp3: Here is the evaluation hierarchy: Parentheses take top priority, followed by multiplication, division, and modulo, then addition and subtraction, comparisons, NOT, AND, and finally OR.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio07.mp3: Notice how AND binds tighter than OR. Without parentheses, the query returns all department ten employees regardless of salary. Adding parentheses around the OR condition ensures both departments must meet the sixty thousand salary threshold.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio08.mp3: Always use parentheses when mixing AND and OR. They cost nothing at runtime, clearly document your intent, and prevent subtle logical bugs in production.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio09.mp3: Subquery modifiers ALL and ANY allow you to compare a single value against an entire list of values returned by a subquery.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio10.mp3: Greater than ANY returns true if a value is greater than at least one item in the list — which is logically equivalent to being greater than the minimum value.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio11.mp3: Greater than ALL is stricter — it returns true only if a value is greater than every item in the list, which means exceeding the maximum value.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio12.mp3: Keep in mind that while ALL and ANY are standard SQL in PostgreSQL and MySQL, SQLite does not support them natively. Emulate them in SQLite using SELECT MIN for ANY, and SELECT MAX for ALL.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio13.mp3: Remember these core equivalences: equals ANY is identical to IN, not equal ALL is identical to NOT IN, greater than ALL matches MAX, and greater than ANY matches MIN.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio14.mp3: When your text data contains actual percent signs or underscores, use the ESCAPE clause to search for them as literal characters rather than wildcards.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio15.mp3: In these examples, the exclamation mark is declared as the escape character. Exclamation percent matches a literal percent sign, and exclamation underscore matches a literal underscore.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio16.mp3: Standard SQL has no default escape character, so always declare one explicitly using single quotes. Popular choices include exclamation mark, backslash, or hash.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio17.mp3: Handling missing data requires care. In SQL, any arithmetic with NULL yields NULL, and any comparison with NULL produces UNKNOWN, filtering the row out.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio18.mp3: Adding salary and commission yields NULL whenever commission is missing. Wrapping commission in COALESCE with zero replaces NULL safely. When filtering, use OR commission IS NULL to explicitly retain missing records.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio19.mp3: Remember that NULL represents an unknown value, not zero. Because NULL equals NULL evaluates to UNKNOWN, always use IS NULL or IS NOT NULL to check for missing values.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio20.mp3: SQL operates on three-valued logic: TRUE, FALSE, or UNKNOWN. The WHERE clause only includes rows where the condition evaluates strictly to TRUE.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio21.mp3: Looking at the truth table, any comparison against NULL yields UNKNOWN. Since WHERE discards UNKNOWN results just like FALSE, missing values are excluded automatically.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio22.mp3: Watch out for the NOT IN trap. If a subquery returns even a single NULL value, NOT IN evaluates to UNKNOWN for all rows, returning an empty result set.
lecture.New_Day4Part1audio23.mp3: To prevent the NOT IN trap, either filter out NULLs in the subquery using IS NOT NULL, or use NOT EXISTS, which handles NULLs safely by design.

questions.New_Day4Question01.mp3: Ready to calculate monthly pay? Retrieve first name, salary, and monthly salary — computed as salary divided by twelve — for all employees.
questions.New_Day4Question01sol.mp3: Let's write this query. First, SELECT first_name, salary, then compute salary divided by twelve point zero AS monthly_salary... FROM employees.
questions.New_Day4Question02.mp3: Time to calculate product profit margins! Calculate gross profit by subtracting cost price from unit price for each product, and order the results by gross profit descending.
questions.New_Day4Question02sol.mp3: Let's code this out. SELECT name, unit_price, cost_price, then calculate unit_price minus cost_price AS gross_profit... FROM products, ORDER BY gross_profit DESC.
questions.New_Day4Question03.mp3: Here's a NULL handling challenge! Compute total compensation by adding salary and commission, using COALESCE to treat NULL commissions as zero.
questions.New_Day4Question03sol.mp3: Let's write the query. SELECT first_name, salary, commission, then add salary plus COALESCE of commission, zero AS total_comp... FROM employees.
questions.New_Day4Question04.mp3: Let's test your operator precedence skills! Find employees in department ten or twenty earning over seventy thousand, using explicit parentheses around the department condition.
questions.New_Day4Question04sol.mp3: Let me write the query with parentheses. SELECT star FROM employees, WHERE open paren department_id equals 10 OR department_id equals 20 close paren... AND salary greater than 70000.
questions.New_Day4Question05.mp3: Can you out-earn the Sales department? Find employees whose salary is greater than every salary in the Sales department, department forty, using a MAX subquery.
questions.New_Day4Question05sol.mp3: Let's build the query. SELECT first_name, salary FROM employees, WHERE salary is greater than open paren SELECT MAX of salary FROM employees WHERE department_id equals 40 close paren.
questions.New_Day4Question06.mp3: Time to apply a price markup! Compute a markup price column representing unit price multiplied by one point one eight for all products.
questions.New_Day4Question06sol.mp3: Let's type this out. SELECT name, and unit_price times 1.18 AS markup_price... FROM products.
questions.New_Day4Question07.mp3: Here's a classic NULL filtering puzzle! Find employees whose commission is greater than five thousand or whose commission is NULL.
questions.New_Day4Question07sol.mp3: Let me write the condition. SELECT first_name, commission FROM employees, WHERE commission is greater than 5000... OR commission IS NULL.
questions.New_Day4Question08.mp3: Let's prevent a divide-by-zero crash! Compute profit margin as unit price minus cost price divided by unit price, using NULLIF to safely handle zero unit prices.
questions.New_Day4Question08sol.mp3: Let me code this safely. SELECT name, open paren unit_price minus cost_price close paren times 1.0 divided by NULLIF of unit_price, 0 AS margin... FROM products.
questions.New_Day4Question09.mp3: Ready for a percentage calculation? Calculate profit percentage for each product, rounded to two decimal places.
questions.New_Day4Question09sol.mp3: Let me write the percentage formula. SELECT name, ROUND of open paren unit_price minus cost_price close paren times 100.0 divided by unit_price, 2 AS profit_pct... FROM products.
questions.New_Day4Question10.mp3: Can you beat at least one Marketing salary? Find employees earning more than at least one employee in Marketing, department thirty, using a MIN subquery.
questions.New_Day4Question10sol.mp3: Let's code the subquery solution. SELECT first_name, salary FROM employees, WHERE salary is greater than open paren SELECT MIN of salary FROM employees WHERE department_id equals 30 close paren.
questions.New_Day4Question11.mp3: Let's put the modulo operator to work! Retrieve products with an even product ID using the modulo operator.
questions.New_Day4Question11sol.mp3: Let's write the modulo filter. SELECT product_id, name FROM products, WHERE product_id modulo 2 equals 0.
questions.New_Day4Question12.mp3: Here's your final inventory valuation challenge! Compute stock value as stock quantity times cost price, returning products where stock value exceeds one hundred thousand.
questions.New_Day4Question12sol.mp3: Let me type the full query. SELECT name, stock_qty times cost_price AS stock_value FROM products, WHERE stock_qty times cost_price is greater than 100000.
